edges.description: OspC-mediated transmission deposits infectious spirochetes in the host dermis, initiating local colonization and dissemination.
Salp15-coated spirochetes resist early humoral clearance at the bite site, enabling establishment of dermal infection and subsequent dissemination.
edges.source: Tick Saliva-Assisted Immune Evasion via Salp15-OspC

edges.description: Disseminating spirochetes deliver lipoproteins and peptidoglycan that drive innate inflammation at colonized tissues.
Centrifugal migration of spirochetes outward through the dermis at the bite site produces the characteristic expanding erythema migrans rash of early localized infection.
Blocking antibody-dependent complement killing allows surviving spirochetes to disseminate despite humoral immunity.
intermediate_mechanisms: Classical-pathway inhibition yields serum resistance, permitting spirochete survival during hematogenous dissemination.
edges.source: Complement Evasion via BBK32-Mediated C1r Inhibition

edges.description: Alternative-pathway evasion sustains spirochete viability in serum, supporting hematogenous spread.
intermediate_mechanisms: Factor H recruitment produces serum resistance that enables spirochete survival in blood during dissemination.
edges.source: Alternative Complement Pathway Evasion via Factor H Recruitment

edges.description: Antigenic variation maintains an immune-evasive, persistent infection that supports continued tissue colonization.
intermediate_mechanisms: Continuous surface-antigen diversification produces immune escape and persistent infection that sustain ongoing dissemination.
edges.source: Antigenic Variation via vlsE Gene Conversion

edges.description: Lipid scavenging and residual biosynthesis maintain the spirochete cell envelope, supporting survival and dissemination in the vertebrate host.
intermediate_mechanisms: Host-lipid-dependent membrane biogenesis and cell-envelope remodeling sustain spirochete viability, enabling survival in host tissues during colonization and dissemination.
edges.source: Host Lipid Scavenging and Membrane Biogenesis

edges.description: Inflammatory cytokines and immune-cell infiltration in and around joints produce the arthralgia that commonly precedes frank arthritis.
Neutrophilic and lymphocytic synovial inflammation driven by Borrelia antigens produces the mono/oligoarticular inflammatory arthritis of Lyme disease.
Immune-mediated cranial neuritis of the facial nerve produces unilateral or bilateral facial palsy.
intermediate_mechanisms: Perineural inflammation of the facial (cranial VII) nerve during early disseminated neuroborreliosis.

edges.description: Inflammation of the cardiac conduction system disrupts AV nodal conduction, producing variable-degree atrioventricular block.
intermediate_mechanisms: Inflammatory infiltration of the myocardium and AV conduction system (Lyme carditis).

edges.description: Inflammatory involvement of the spinal cord during acute neuroborreliosis produces myelitis.
CNS inflammation in neuroborreliosis produces a subcortical/reversible dementia syndrome that improves with antibiotic therapy.
intermediate_mechanisms: CNS inflammation/encephalopathy from intrathecal Borrelia infection and immune response.

edges.description: Systemic pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF) produce the profound constitutional fatigue seen across disease stages.
Sustained antigen-driven inflammation is proposed to break self-tolerance in genetically predisposed hosts.
Tissue damage and inflammation from viable or nonviable spirochetes are a proposed driver of persistent post-treatment symptoms.
Post-infectious autoimmune responses are one of the leading proposed mechanisms underlying persistent post-treatment symptoms.
Post-infectious autoimmunity drives antibiotic-refractory Lyme arthritis that persists despite eradication of viable spirochetes.
intermediate_mechanisms: HLA-DRB1-restricted autoreactive T-cell responses sustaining synovial inflammation after spirochete clearance.

edges.description: Post-infectious autoimmunity is a proposed trigger for the rare autoimmune blistering (EBA-like) skin lesions reported after Lyme disease.
intermediate_mechanisms: Autoantibody-mediated subepidermal blistering (epidermolysis bullosa acquisita-like) triggered after Lyme infection.

edges.description: Persistent immune dysregulation underlies the chronic fatigue that is a core symptom of post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome.

nodes.description: Borrelia burgdorferi expresses complement regulator-acquiring surface proteins (CRASPs) such as CspA (CRASP-1), along with CspZ and OspE-family paralogs, that bind the host alternative-pathway regulators factor H and factor H-like protein 1 (FH/FHL-1). Surface-bound FH/FHL-1 limits C3 deposition and membrane-attack-complex formation, conferring resistance to complement-mediated killing in human serum. This pathway complements the BBK32-mediated classical-pathway block.
nodes.id: Alternative Complement Pathway Evasion via Factor H Recruitment

evidence.reference_title: CspA-mediated binding of human factor H inhibits complement deposition and confers serum resistance in Borrelia burgdorferi.
evidence.snippet: the CspA-mediated binding of human FH confers serum resistance by directly inhibiting complement deposition on the surface of B. burgdorferi.

nodes.description: The vlsE locus undergoes gene conversion that diversifies Borrelia surface antigens, helping the pathogen evade host immune recognition during dissemination and persistent infection.
nodes.id: Antigenic Variation via vlsE Gene Conversion

evidence.reference_title: Breaking a barrier: In trans vlsE recombination and genetic manipulation of the native vlsE gene of the Lyme disease pathogen.
evidence.snippet: Borrelia burgdorferi, the Lyme disease pathogen, has evolved an intricate antigenic variation mechanism to evade the host immune response, enabling its dissemination, persistence, and pathogenicity.

nodes.description: Joint pain, particularly affecting large joints such as the knee, commonly precedes frank arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease among Patients at an Ambulatory Unit in a Highly Endemic Country: Lithuania.
evidence.snippet: Clinical symptoms were as follows: arthralgia (33.2%), general weakness (19%), head pain (13.1%), myalgia (11.2%), fever (6.2%), joint swelling (5.9%), head dizziness (5.1%), nervus facialis neuropathy (2.4%), and radiculitis (2.0%).

nodes.description: Inflammatory arthritis, typically monoarticular or oligoarticular, affecting large joints especially the knee. Occurs in approximately 60% of untreated patients.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease among Patients at an Ambulatory Unit in a Highly Endemic Country: Lithuania.
evidence.snippet: Lyme arthritis mainly manifested among middle-aged women as an oligoarthritis, mostly affecting the knee joint.

nodes.description: Cardiac involvement including AV block (first, second, or third degree) and myocarditis. Lyme carditis occurs in 1-5% of untreated patients.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme disease presenting as complete heart block in a young man: Case report and review of pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: the most common clinical manifestation is AV block, which can be acute in onset and can rapidly progress to complete heart block.

nodes.description: Prolonged exposure of the host immune system to Borrelia antigens can drive post-infectious autoimmunity, with reported triggers of systemic autoimmune disease after Lyme borreliosis.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Borreliosis as a Trigger for Autoimmune Disease.
evidence.snippet: long-term exposure of the host's immune system to spirochetes can contribute to the development of chronic autoimmune disease de novo.

nodes.description: Borrelia burgdorferi maintains its peptidoglycan cell wall by penicillin-binding protein (PBP) transpeptidase cross-linking. This essential, bacteria-specific reaction is the target of the amoxicillin and ceftriaxone used to treat Lyme disease; beta-lactam acylation of the PBP active site halts cross-linking and is bactericidal against disseminating spirochetes. CNS penetration of ceftriaxone is what extends this mechanism to neuroborreliosis.

evidence.snippet: although glycan chain polymerization occurs in the absence of transpeptidation (for example, in the presence of penicillin), efficient transpeptidation requires ongoing GTase reactions

nodes.description: Borrelia burgdorferi depends on 70S-ribosome translation. Doxycycline, the first-line agent for early localized and early disseminated Lyme disease, binds the 30S subunit and arrests bacterial protein synthesis — a ribosomal target distinct from the PBP target of the beta-lactam alternatives.

evidence.snippet: The ribosome is one of the main antibiotic targets in the bacterial cell.

nodes.description: Autoimmune blistering lesions (epidermolysis bullosa acquisita-like) following Lyme infection; reported after atypical erythema migrans.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease Causing Complex Bullous Lesions: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: developed acquired epidermolysis bullosa (EBA) following an LD diagnosis. Initially, the patient presented with atypical erythema migrans, which later evolved into multiple bullous lesions

nodes.description: The Borrelia burgdorferi surface lipoprotein BBK32 binds C1r, the initiator protease of the classical complement pathway. This attenuates antibody-dependent complement activation and protects infectious Borrelia from complement-mediated killing in human serum.
nodes.id: Complement Evasion via BBK32-Mediated C1r Inhibition

evidence.reference_title: BBK32 attenuates antibody-dependent complement-mediated killing of infectious Borreliella burgdorferi isolates.
evidence.snippet: BBK32 binds to C1r, the initiator protease of the classical pathway of complement, and protects B. burgdorferi from complement-mediated killing following exposure to normal human serum.

nodes.description: Subcortical or reversible dementia presentations of neuroborreliosis that improve with antibiotic therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Secondary dementia due to Lyme neuroborreliosis.
evidence.snippet: Dementia-like syndromes are rare manifestations of Lyme neuroborreliosis... The response to 2-4 weeks of antibiotic treatment with ceftriaxone was excellent.

evidence.reference_title: A Case of Reversible Dementia? Dementia vs Delirium in Lyme Disease.
evidence.snippet: Lyme disease is an uncommon cause of reversible dementia... the brilliant clinical improvement after the appropriate antimicrobial therapy is strongly suggestive for a diagnosis of neuroborreliosis.

nodes.description: Characteristic expanding circular rash at the site of tick bite, often with central clearing (bull's-eye appearance). Present in 70-80% of cases.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease among Patients at an Ambulatory Unit in a Highly Endemic Country: Lithuania.
evidence.snippet: Results: In total, 1005 patients were enrolled with the following prevalence of clinical syndromes: erythema migrans (EM), 945 (94.02%); Lyme arthritis, 32 (3.18%); neuroborreliosis, 23 (2.28%); Lyme carditis, 4 (0.39%); and acrodermatitis, 1 (0.09%).

nodes.description: Unilateral or bilateral facial nerve palsy (Bell's palsy) in early disseminated disease, occurring in approximately 5% of untreated patients.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease among Patients at an Ambulatory Unit in a Highly Endemic Country: Lithuania.
evidence.snippet: The main symptoms were the following: nervus facialis neuropathy (47.8%), myalgia (39%), head pain (26%), and radiculitis (21%).

nodes.description: Profound fatigue is common in all stages of Lyme disease and may persist in post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease among Patients at an Ambulatory Unit in a Highly Endemic Country: Lithuania.
evidence.snippet: Clinical symptoms were as follows: arthralgia (33.2%), general weakness (19%), head pain (13.1%), myalgia (11.2%), fever (6.2%), joint swelling (5.9%), head dizziness (5.1%), nervus facialis neuropathy (2.4%), and radiculitis (2.0%).

nodes.description: Borrelia burgdorferi is a metabolically compromised spirochete (~1.5 Mbp genome) that cannot survive without host-derived lipids: it lacks de novo sterol synthesis and most lipid-biosynthetic capacity, requires exogenous cholesterol for growth and viability, and must scavenge lipids from the tick and vertebrate host to build and remodel its LPS-free, lipoprotein-rich cell envelope during the enzootic cycle. A multi-platform untargeted and targeted LC-MS/GC-MS lipidomic compendium detected more than 500 lipids in B. burgdorferi, the majority sourced from the environment through apparently discriminatory uptake (some species depleted 600-1500-fold during growth). The pathogen organizes neutral lipids — cholesteryl esters and triglycerides — into distinct intracellular foci (the first visual/molecular evidence of lipid-droplet-like storage in this minimal bacterium), and incorporates host sphingolipids and ceramides, with phosphatidylcholine established as a principal membrane constituent alongside cholesterol and cholesterol galactolipids (AcGal) that form cholesterol/sphingolipid lipid rafts. Unexpectedly, the spirochete also biosynthesizes predominantly eukaryotic lipids: four lysosomal bis(monoacylglycerol)phosphate (BMP) species (proposed roles in membrane plasticity/stress adaptation), phosphatidylglycerol and phosphatidylethanolamine, the plant galactolipid monogalactosyl diacylglycerol (MGDG; against which late-stage Lyme patients mount an antibody response), an acylated glycosylated phytosterol (acylhexosylcampesterol from scavenged campesterol), and the plant-type anionic sulfolipid sulfoquinovosyl diacylglycerol (SQDG). Structural-homology modeling against Arabidopsis SQD1/SQD2 and heterologous E. coli reconstitution identified two interdependent B. burgdorferi enzymes, BB0444 and BB0454, as sufficient for SQDG biosynthesis (BB0454 also makes MGDG); homologs are broadly conserved across spirochetes and >30 Borrelia species. This "lipid chimera" strategy of intensive scavenging plus niche biosynthesis is essential for envelope integrity and persistence, nominating lipid acquisition, sulfolipid synthesis, and neutral- lipid storage as candidate antimicrobial targets.
nodes.id: Host Lipid Scavenging and Membrane Biogenesis

biological_processes: cholesteryl ester and triglyceride storage in intracellular foci

evidence.reference_title: A lipid compendium of a metabolically compromised bacterium provides insights into lipid acquisition, biosynthesis, and metabolism.
evidence.snippet: belongs to a class of metabolically compromised bacteria that cannot survive without host-derived lipids

evidence.reference_title: A lipid compendium of a metabolically compromised bacterium provides insights into lipid acquisition, biosynthesis, and metabolism.
evidence.snippet: The analysis detected more than 500 lipids in

evidence.reference_title: A lipid compendium of a metabolically compromised bacterium provides insights into lipid acquisition, biosynthesis, and metabolism.
evidence.snippet: the bacterium selectively accumulates certain lipids while excluding others, suggesting discriminatory uptake. These include cholesteryl esters and triglycerides that are organized in foci within the pathogen

evidence.reference_title: A lipid compendium of a metabolically compromised bacterium provides insights into lipid acquisition, biosynthesis, and metabolism.
evidence.snippet: the pathogen also synthesizes predominantly eukaryotic lipids such as the lysosomal bis(monoacylglycerol)phosphate and the plant glycolipid sulfoquinovosyl diacylglycerol (SQDG)

evidence.reference_title: A lipid compendium of a metabolically compromised bacterium provides insights into lipid acquisition, biosynthesis, and metabolism.
evidence.snippet: highlights lipid acquisition and synthesis pathways as potentially critical for pathogen survival

evidence.reference_title: A lipid compendium of a metabolically compromised bacterium provides insights into lipid acquisition, biosynthesis, and metabolism.
evidence.snippet: The biosynthesis of the latter is carried out by enzymes that exhibit structural homology to plant oxidoreductases and galactosyltransferases

nodes.description: The host immune response to Borrelia antigens contributes to tissue damage. Spirochetal lipoproteins and peptidoglycan trigger robust innate immune activation with production of IL-6, IL-8, TNF, and chemokines (CCL3/CCL4). Persistent inflammation in joints may continue even after spirochete clearance.

evidence.reference_title: Trauma-related Lyme arthritis.
evidence.snippet: Aspiration of the left knee joint revealed a white cell count of 21.0×109/L (83% neutrophils) with negative Gram stain and culture. However, Lyme PCR was positive and accompanied by serologies consistent with Lyme arthritis.

nodes.description: Recommended for neurological Lyme disease (neuroborreliosis), Lyme carditis with high-degree AV block, and some cases of Lyme arthritis. Duration typically 14-28 days.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease Presenting With Interesting Neurological Features of Weakness and Hyporeflexia: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: The patient's symptoms resolved quickly with a four-day inpatient course of IV ceftriaxone followed by 10 days of oral doxycycline.

nodes.description: Inflammatory spinal cord involvement as a rare manifestation of acute neuroborreliosis, sometimes with mild clinical symptoms despite marked MRI edema.

evidence.reference_title: Isolated Cervical Myelitis in Lyme Disease: A Rare Manifestation of Acute Neuroborreliosis.
evidence.snippet: We report a case of acute neuroborreliosis that manifested as extended isolated cervical myelitis.

nodes.description: Alternative first-line agent for early Lyme disease, preferred in children under 8 years and pregnant women. Typical dose is 500mg three times daily.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme disease in pregnancy: case report and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: We describe a favorable outcome in a 42-year-old woman who developed Lyme disease in the third trimester and was treated with a full course of oral amoxicillin.

nodes.description: First-line treatment for early localized and early disseminated Lyme disease in adults. Typical dose is 100mg twice daily for 10-21 days. Also provides prophylaxis for tick bites in endemic areas.

evidence.reference_title: The clinical assessment, treatment, and prevention of lyme disease, human granulocytic anaplasmosis, and babesiosis: clinical practice guidelines by the Infectious Diseases Society of America.
evidence.snippet: Tables list the doses and durations of antimicrobial therapy recommended for treatment and prevention of Lyme disease

nodes.description: In an estimated 10-20% of appropriately treated patients, symptoms such as fatigue, pain, and cognitive complaints persist for more than six months (post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome, PTLDS). The mechanism is not fully established; proposed drivers include an autoimmune response to spirochete-induced tissue damage and inflammation, broader immune dysfunction, and persistent viable or nonviable antigen. This node is a terminal post-infectious state rather than ongoing high-burden active infection in most cases.

evidence.reference_title: What Makes It Tick: Exploring the Mechanisms of Post-treatment Lyme Disease Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We propose that ongoing PTLDS symptoms seem to be related to an autoimmune response to the tissue damage and inflammation caused by the viable or nonviable spirochete pathogen.

evidence.reference_title: CCL19 as a Chemokine Risk Factor for Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome: a Prospective Clinical Cohort Study.
evidence.snippet: Receiver operating characteristic analysis identified a CCL19 cutoff of >111.67 pg/ml at 1 month following treatment completion to be 82% sensitive and 83% specific for later PTLDS.

nodes.description: Borrelia burgdorferi is transmitted through Ixodes tick bites. The spirochete disseminates from the skin through the bloodstream to multiple organ systems including joints, heart, and nervous system. Surface adhesins like BBK32 and decorin-binding proteins (DbpA/DbpB) facilitate tissue colonization, while OspC is upregulated at the tick-feeding/early-mammalian interface and is required for early infectivity.

evidence.reference_title: Lyme Disease among Patients at an Ambulatory Unit in a Highly Endemic Country: Lithuania.
evidence.snippet: The clinical manifestations of Lyme disease range from a localized infection, for example, erythema migrans (EM) to a disseminated neurological or rheumatological infection, cardiac involvement, or even chronic disease.

nodes.description: During transmission, Borrelia burgdorferi co-opts components of Ixodes tick saliva to establish infection at the bite site. The spirochete selectively enhances tick expression of the 15-kDa salivary protein Salp15, which binds the spirochetal surface lipoprotein OspC and coats the organism. Salp15 (and its I. ricinus homologue Iric1) shields Borrelia from antibody-mediated killing and complement, giving the spirochete a marked infectivity advantage in the mammalian host; the bacterium thereby exploits a secreted arthropod protein to colonize the vertebrate dermis.
nodes.id: Tick Saliva-Assisted Immune Evasion via Salp15-OspC

evidence.reference_title: The Lyme disease agent exploits a tick protein to infect the mammalian host.
evidence.snippet: Salp15 was then shown to adhere to the spirochaete, both in vitro and in vivo, and specifically interacted with B. burgdorferi outer surface protein C. The binding of Salp15 protected B. burgdorferi from antibody-mediated killing in vitro and provided spirochaetes with a marked advantage when they were inoculated into naive mice

evidence.reference_title: Strong interactions between Salp15 homologues from the tick I. ricinus and distinct types of the outer surface OspC protein from Borrelia.
evidence.snippet: we show in this study that also Iric2 and Iric3 are capable of OspC binding with high affinity

nodes.description: Borrelia burgdorferi is maintained in an enzootic cycle between Ixodes ticks and small-mammal reservoirs such as the white-footed mouse; larval ticks acquire spirochetes by feeding on infected reservoirs and transmit them transstadially to the nymphal stage that bites humans. In the unfed tick midgut the spirochete expresses OspA (midgut adhesion) but not OspC. During the blood meal, host- and temperature-derived signals trigger a reciprocal antigenic switch in which OspA is downregulated and OspC is upregulated, accompanying spirochete migration from the midgut to the salivary glands and transmission into the vertebrate dermis; OspC expression peaks around 48 h of tick attachment and is required for early mammalian infectivity.

evidence.reference_title: Temporal changes in outer surface proteins A and C of the lyme disease-associated spirochete, Borrelia burgdorferi, during the chain of infection in ticks and mice.
evidence.snippet: B. burgdorferi expresses outer surface protein A (OspA) but not OspC when residing in the midgut of unfed ticks. However, after ticks feed on blood, some spirochetes stop making OspA and express OspC.

evidence.reference_title: Of ticks, mice and men: understanding the dual-host lifestyle of Lyme disease spirochaetes.
evidence.snippet: we integrate this large body of information into a cohesive picture of the molecular and cellular events that transpire as Lyme disease spirochaetes transit between their arthropod and vertebrate hosts during the enzootic cycle.
